Characteristics: Hunter-gatherer gift economies enforcing reciprocity
notes: Maximum E and D case. The ridicule/levelling mechanism is explicit D. Hxaro exchange networks maintain alliance without hierarchy.

Characteristics: Egalitarian burn-rotate proto-cities (15k population)
notes: Deliberate burn-and-rebuild cycle is institutionalised R. No evidence of chiefs or administrative apparatus. Low P — prestige exists but not institutionalised competition. EDR corrected upward from auto-code.

Characteristics: Ziggurat-centered redistributive economies
notes: Temple as redistributive hub (high L, rising A). Priests hold S via ritual monopoly not military. D low — temple obligations binding. Corrects auto-code which underweighted A and S.

Characteristics: Regional governors (nomarchs) under divine pharaoh
notes: Nomarchs are delegated sovereignty under pharaoh — S is high but shared. A moderate via taxation records. E very low — land-bound peasantry. Predynastic period before full Old Kingdom lock-in.
succession_changes: rotation->hereditary (-2055): reconsolidation — Mentuhotep II reunifies; pharaonic hereditary restored; EDR partially suppressed again

Characteristics: Assembly of nobles to depose kings (Telipinu Edict)
notes: The Panku is an early institutional D mechanism — formal council with power to constrain the king. Bronze Age Collapse eliminates it.

Characteristics: Feudal decentralization under ritualized monarchy
notes: Decentralised feudalism with genuine D mechanism: Mandate of Heaven legitimises overthrow of bad rulers. More D than auto-code suggests. Western Zhou more decentralised than Eastern.

Characteristics: Harbor city-states ruled by purple-dye cartels
notes: City-state merchants: high P (commercial competition), meaningful E (can relocate ships/capital), good A (trade records, contracts). Auto-code was close but E slightly high — exit possible but costly.

Characteristics: Kin-based labor reciprocity and terraced farming
notes: Ayllu is kin-based reciprocal labour — high EDR at community level. S low: no chiefs above ayllu level in pre-Inca form. Corrects auto-code which defaulted to mid values.

Characteristics: Professional army enforces king's rule through terror
notes: Near-maximum S. Systematic terror as policy instrument. D=0: refusal = death by impalement. Deportation policy eliminates E. Auto-code had A too low — Assyrian annals and census records are sophisticated.

Characteristics: City-kings elected from oligarchic leagues
Regional governors (satraps) under King of Kings
notes: Already hand-coded in extended dataset as Satrapy System — this is the same entry from v2. Correcting to match. High A (royal road, satrap reports), high S (King of Kings), low EDR.

Characteristics: Mixed government with consuls, senate, and assemblies
notes: The Roman transition from Republic to Empire is a textbook lock-in sequence: P (competitive politics) generates military S, which then eliminates D and R.
succession_changes: elective->hereditary (-27): P_to_S — Octavian concentration of imperium; cursus honorum competition generates military S that eliminates D

Characteristics: Direct citizen rule with sortition and ostracism
notes: Distinct from generic Greek Democracy entry. P very high: politics as competitive performance (rhetoric, drama, courts). D high via ostracism (institutionalised D against dangerous individuals). E moderate: metics can leave, citizens less so.
succession_changes: elective->appointment (-411): external_shock — Sicilian Expedition disaster enables Council of 400 | appointment->elective (-403): elite_reform — Thrasybulus restoration; pre-existing civic D memory enables rapid reversal | elective->appointment (-322): external_shock — Macedonian conquest; Demetrius of Phalerum installed

Characteristics: Standardized totalitarianism with collective punishment
notes: Maximum lock-in. Collective punishment eliminates D at household level. Standardised weights/script/axles = extreme L and I. Shorter than any dynasty: fragility at maximum SAP confirmed by near-immediate collapse.
succession_changes: hereditary->appointment (-206): collapse — Chen Sheng rebellion; Han adopts modified Legalist admin with merit overlay

Characteristics: Divine kings competing via calendar rituals and wars
notes: High P (ritual performance IS governance), but also high S (divine king). A significant via glyphic records and calendar. Not the auto-coded mid values. Classic Maya is extreme P not suppressed P.

Characteristics: Decentralized Hindu renaissance under nominal emperors
notes: Genuinely decentralised: Gupta emperors ruled through existing local structures. Cultural florescence = high R (Buddhism, mathematics, drama all flourishing simultaneously). Auto-code underestimated EDR. EDR revised after reliability exercise: caste system constraints on E, D, R were underweighted in original coding. E=0.40 (caste mobility very limited), D=0.30 (Brahmanical authority hard to refuse), R=0.45 (cultural florescence is real but within caste constraints).

Characteristics: Farmer-soldier provinces with military governors
notes: High A (theme system is administrative innovation — military-agricultural census). High S (emperor). Low EDR. Auto-code was far too generous — this is a centralising military-bureaucratic state. P revised to 0.45 after reliability exercise.

Characteristics: Oligarchic merchant state with elaborate election lotteries
notes: The elaborate ballot systems (ballotta) are institutionalised P. High A via trade records and state archives. D moderate: councils constrain Doge but citizens have limited voice. More A and less E than auto-code.

Characteristics: Itinerant judges memorizing tribal laws at Things
notes: High E (Viking mobility is definitional). Oral law = low I but meaningful D (law-speaker recites, community validates). Auto-code was close. Confidence 2 — this is a functional dimension of the broader Norse Thing system.

Characteristics: Elective emperor + prince-bishop power-sharing
notes: The HRE is less centralised than the auto-code suggests. S is low (emperor genuinely constrained by princes). P high (competitive election of emperor). R higher than auto-code: the constitutional complexity allowed novel institutional arrangements. Corrects toward our hand-coded Holy Roman Empire entry.

Characteristics: Hydraulic empire where kings embodied Hindu gods
notes: The hydraulic state: A is built around irrigation management. High L via rice surplus. Collapse linked to climate stress hitting the hydraulic infrastructure.

Characteristics: Seafaring corporations with elected admiral-governors
notes: Ur assemblies (local councils) gave genuine D at village level even under imperial structure. Naval corporations (nagarams) had elected leadership — P meaningful. More balanced than auto-code mid-values.

Characteristics: Farmer-lawyers resolving disputes through saga precedents
notes: No state, no executive — the goðar are private contractors of dispute resolution. E near-maximum (emigration is normal). D encoded: you can simply refuse arbitration (at reputational cost). High R: institutional innovation is the main political activity (sagas are governance records).

Characteristics: World's oldest parliamentary body with outdoor deliberations
notes: Same tradition as Commonwealth but with more institutionalised assembly. Seasonality high — the Althing meeting IS the governance event. Very high EDR. Auto-code significantly underestimated.

Characteristics: World's oldest parliament now fully geothermal-powered
notes: Modern Iceland: high I (e-government), meaningful D (referendums), high E (small open economy). The geothermal energy framing is superficial — code the governance structure, not the energy policy.

Characteristics: Continuous parliamentary assembly with outdoor moot hill
notes: Continuous parliament with outdoor Tynwald Hill reading (high seasonality for the ceremony). Crown dependency: genuine D via legislative autonomy from UK. E high — small jurisdiction.

Characteristics: Centralized land census enforcing knight-service quotas
notes: Domesday Book is the highest pre-modern I event in English history — total legibility exercise. S high (conquest state). D near zero. Auto-code had this right but confidence was 1.
succession_changes: appointment->elective (1215): elite_reform — Magna Carta; baronial coalition coerces John; customary D enables constraint | elective->elective (1295): elite_reform — Model Parliament; commons added to elected representation

Characteristics: State-run shipyard with worker-patriot collectives
notes: Arsenal workers (arsenalotti) had collective identity and state privileges — more D than typical guilds. Embedded in Venetian Republic: A high via state management. Narrower entry than Venetian Republic proper.

Characteristics: Hydraulic city-temple network under god-kings
notes: Hydraulic state: A built around irrigation control. High S (devaraja). Very low EDR. Auto-code had S too low and EDR too high. This is essentially the same system as our hand-coded Khmer Devaraja entry — should align closely.

Characteristics: Sacred monarchy with guild-based bronze bureaucracy
notes: Oba is divine sovereign (high S). Guild-based bronze casting is proto-bureaucracy (A moderate). D extremely low — Oba's word is sacred law. Auto-code was close but EDR too generous.

Characteristics: Feudal contract limiting royal power via council
notes: Magna Carta IS a D mechanism — formalises baronial right to refuse royal overreach. S constrained (not eliminated). P high: competitive baronial politics. EDR higher than auto-code. Short-lived as a pure form but influential.
succession_changes: elective->hereditary (1265): collapse — Second Barons War; Henry III reasserts hereditary prerogative after de Montfort defeat

Characteristics: Crusader-monk state ruling via brick castle networks
notes: Military-monastic state: high S (Grand Master), significant A (castle administration, land records). D near zero for subjects. Auto-code had it right. Elective Grand Master is P but within very narrow pool.

Characteristics: Mounted postal network as de facto administration
notes: Yam (postal relay) is an administrative innovation that drives A upward. S high (Khan). Kurultai tradition gives some P (competitive leadership selection). EDR low but slightly higher than Qin — subjects had more residual customary freedom.

Characteristics: Unique diarchy with French president + Spanish bishop as co-rulers
notes: Unique constitutional arrangement with genuine D (co-princes balance each other, citizens can appeal to either). High E (tiny jurisdiction, open borders). Auto-code underestimated EDR significantly.

Characteristics: Wealth-qualified patricians ruling via elaborate lotteries
notes: Late Venetian Republic: extremely high A (state archives, trade records, intelligence network). The Serrata (1297) locks the patriciate but within it P is very high via elaborate ballot systems. More A than earlier Venetian entries.
succession_changes: elective->elective (1297): elite_reform — Serrata closes Great Council; sortition retained but eligibility permanently restricted; D decreases within type

Characteristics: Merchant guild federation with extraterritorial trading posts
notes: Federation without sovereignty — no army, no territory. High E by design (cities can leave). D high: member cities retain veto. P high: commercial competition drives governance. Significantly more EDR than auto-code.

Characteristics: Merchant oligarchies with shared trade laws
notes: Similar to Hanseatic League but more territorially rooted — slightly less E and more S than the full League. Auto-code too low on EDR.

Characteristics: Population registers enforcing corvée labor quotas
notes: The Yellow Register is one of history's most ambitious L+I projects: every household, every plot, every obligation recorded. Near-maximum A. D near zero. Peasant revolt collapse is the fragile-regime prediction confirmed.

Characteristics: Meritocratic civil service under royal secretariats
notes: Korean Confucian state: very high A (extensive censorate, exam records). Sarim scholars provide limited D through remonstrance tradition. Auto-code significantly underestimated SAP and overestimated EDR.

Characteristics: Military confederacy with flower wars and sacrifice
notes: Near-maximum SAP case with partial E preservation via calpulli system. The combination of high S+A+P with ritual warfare as population control is distinctive.

Characteristics: Religious autonomies under centralized Muslim rule
notes: Matches our hand-coded Ottoman Empire entry. The millet system preserves partial E within religious communities (you can appeal to your own millet courts) but overall EDR is low. Auto-code had it roughly right for EDR but SAP too low.

Characteristics: Dynastic union of disparate kingdoms under one crown
notes: The composite structure gives more EDR than a unitary empire: each kingdom retains its own laws, estates, and customs. R meaningful — novel constitutional arrangements constantly negotiated. Auto-code underestimated EDR. P revised to 0.55 after reliability exercise.

Characteristics: Merchant oligarchy with provincial veto powers
notes: High A (VOC records, state debt instruments, census). P high (regent oligarchy competition). E meaningful — commercial republic, capital can move. D via provincial veto (any province can block). Significantly more EDR than auto-code.

Characteristics: All-female regiments (Mino) with king-veto power
notes: The Mino (Agojie) are institutionalised female sovereignty — unique D mechanism: king's decisions required Mino ratification in some accounts. S high but qualified. Auto-code underestimated A (Dahomey had sophisticated census and trade records).

Characteristics: Ethnic-military caste hierarchy under Confucian bureaucracy
notes: High SAP: banner system stratifies society (A), Qing emperor has high S, examination competition is P. I very high (Yellow Registers continued, expanded). EDR near-zero. Taiping Rebellion is fragile-regime collapse.

Characteristics: Constitutional monarchy with party-based ministerial rule
notes: High A (welfare state bureaucracy, electoral register). High P (competitive party politics). High EDR: exit (emigration legal and common), D (elections, judicial review, free press), R (constitutional conventions can be changed). Auto-code significantly underestimated A and EDR.

Characteristics: Technocratic empire with standardized exams and titles
notes: Napoleon as sovereign (very high S). Extensive A (Code Napoléon, prefect system, cadastral survey). D near zero despite meritocratic rhetoric — opposition = exile or death. Auto-code had R=0.05 which is right.

Characteristics: Direct democracy with rotating executive and veto points
notes: Maximum D: mandatory referendums, citizen initiative, cantonal veto. Very high E. R high: constitutional changes routine via referendum. More EDR than auto-code. A higher than auto-code — sophisticated federal bureaucracy.

Characteristics: Microstate with citizen-initiated referendums and princely veto
notes: Microstate with genuine D (citizen initiatives, referendums). Princely veto is S but constitutionally constrained. High E (open borders, tiny population). Auto-code was reasonable but slightly underestimated EDR.

Characteristics: Westernizing technocrats ruling through emperor
notes: Deliberate state-building: A was the primary project (census, conscription, legal codes). Emperor is S-anchor but Genrō oligarchs hold real power (high P within narrow elite). EDR low — lower than auto-code. I very high (Meiji statistical revolution).

Characteristics: Revolutionary socialist direct democracy (72-day experiment
notes: 72-day experiment: very high R (deliberate institutional invention), high D (recall of delegates), high P (revolutionary competitive politics). S near zero — no monopoly on violence. Auto-code had SAP wrong (defaulted to democracy profile). EDR should be very high.

Characteristics: Voluntary socialist communes with rotating leadership
notes: Voluntary communalism: maximum E (you can leave), high D (assembly decisions), high R (each kibbutz experiments with its own structure). S near zero. Auto-code had high SAP which is wrong — kibbutz is explicitly non-hierarchical.

Characteristics: Ethno-territorial autonomies under centralized party rule
notes: Near-maximum SAP: party sovereignty, Gosplan administration, P suppressed except within party. D effectively zero (dissent = gulag). E: internal passports system prevents movement. Auto-code massively underestimated SAP and overestimated EDR. R marginally revised upward from 0.05 to 0.08 after reliability exercise: samizdat culture and unofficial intellectual life existed but were systematically persecuted. Kept near-zero. E and D held at original conservative values. P revised to 0.55 after reliability exercise.

Characteristics: Meritocratic one-party state with predictive governance
notes: High A (data governance, housing allocation, CPF). S high (PAP dominance). D very low (no meaningful opposition). E: interesting case — physical exit is easy (open economy) but political exit unavailable. L and I very high.

Characteristics: Oil-funded welfare state with transparency mechanisms
notes: High EDR: exit very easy, strong judicial/press D, constitutional R well-established. A high (welfare state). S very low. The oil fund is an L mechanism that paradoxically funds EDR rather than suppressing it — important counter-case to L→lock-in sequence.

Characteristics: Supranational technocracy with subsidiarity principle
notes: High A (regulations, directives, ECJ). S low (member states retain sovereignty). D: member state veto in Council. E: Article 50 (Brexit) confirms exit is possible. R: treaty revision is possible but hard. Auto-code underestimated A and overestimated S.

Characteristics: E-governance with blockchain-secured citizen participation
notes: Maximum I (X-Road digital infrastructure, e-residency, blockchain records). S very low. D high (digital participation tools, strong press freedom). E very high (e-residency enables exit from physical jurisdiction). Auto-code had A too low and SAP wrong.

Characteristics: Semi-sedentary hunter-gatherers; seasonal aggregation/dispersal; evidence of feasting and prestige burial but no fixed hierarchy; high mobility
notes: Key G&W case: demonstrates deliberate oscillation between hierarchical aggregation and egalitarian dispersal. Natufian feasts show P without institutionalisation.

Characteristics: Early agricultural communities; communal storage; no evidence of differential wealth; skull plastering suggests ancestor veneration without hierarchy
notes: Transition point: agriculture begins but hierarchy has not yet emerged. Critical for testing whether agriculture alone drives lock-in.

Characteristics: Larger villages; long-distance obsidian trade; plastered skulls and ancestor cults; first evidence of unequal storage; possible ritual specialists
notes: D begins to decline as ritual specialists gain authority. E still relatively high — villages split frequently. Schismogenesis visible in settlement patterns.

Characteristics: ~8000 inhabitants; no streets or public buildings; no palaces or temples; equal-sized dwellings; entry through roofs; evidence of shared ritual but no institutional authority
notes: G&W's primary example of large-scale egalitarian urbanism. Low S and A at significant scale directly challenges stadial model.

Characteristics: Proto-urban settlements along rivers; temple as redistribution centre; first administrative tokens; population aggregation; early craft specialisation
notes: The administrative transition: tokens are proto-writing used for surplus tracking. A begins rising. Temple as institution starts reducing R.

Characteristics: Emergence of kingship; palace distinct from temple; royal burials (Ur); military expansion; writing shifts from accounting to royal propaganda
notes: The lock-in transition. S surges. E and D drop sharply. The pair Early/Late Uruk is the best documented instance of the freedom-suppression sequence.

Characteristics: Centralised divine monarchy; pyramid construction as sovereignty display; no standing army initially; nome governors as proto-bureaucracy; limited literacy
notes: High-S anchor case. First Intermediate Period collapse restores local E and D — nome governors become autonomous. Classic Tainter dynamic.
succession_changes: hereditary->rotation (-2181): collapse — 4.2kya drought event + admin overextension; nome governors de facto independent

Characteristics: Reunification after First Intermediate Period; increased bureaucratic complexity; professional army; literary tradition; more constrained pharaonic power
notes: Post-collapse restoration shows A increasing relative to S — the bureaucratic class gained ground during the fragmentation period.

Characteristics: Cities of ~40000; elaborate drainage/sanitation; standardised weights; no palaces or royal tombs; no weapons caches; evidence of long-distance trade; script undeciphered
notes: G&W's most enigmatic case. High A (standardisation, weights) without apparent S. Script may reveal more but current evidence suggests unprecedented urban equality.

Characteristics: Divine kingship; oracle bones as proto-administrative record; ancestor worship as political legitimacy; human sacrifice at royal burials; bronze ritual monopoly
notes: High-S with proto-A. Oracle bones are I in service of sovereignty rather than administration proper. Contrast with Zhou feudalism that follows.

Characteristics: Early walled city; evidence of initial hierarchy then apparent levelling; astronomical observatory; possible egalitarian reform period mid-occupation
notes: G&W discuss as possible example of egalitarian reversal — hierarchy established then dismantled. Critical for R (arrangement freedom) argument.

Characteristics: Tribal republics (gaṇa-saṃghas); assembly-based governance; Vajjian Confederacy and Licchavi republic as peak examples; warrior aristocracy with deliberative councils
notes: The Indian republican tradition that Buddhism emerged within. High P (kshatriya competition) with meaningful D. Absorption into Mauryan Empire is a lock-in event.

Characteristics: First Mesoamerican complex society; colossal heads (ruler portraits); long-distance jade/obsidian trade; no deciphered writing; uncertain political structure
notes: P-dominant emergence case: colossal heads suggest personalised sovereignty display. May be the P→S transition in action.

Characteristics: Peripatetic tribes; assembly governance (comitia); elected war-leaders; druidic priesthood as ritual authority; high geographic mobility; deliberate non-urbanisation
notes: G&W discuss Celtic deliberate avoidance of urbanism as schismogenesis against Mediterranean model. High E and D maintained as political value.

Characteristics: Horse-mounted pastoral nomads; elected war-leaders; kurgan burial with wealth display; no fixed territory; resistance to Persian administrative incorporation
notes: High-E extreme case. Persian attempts to incorporate failed precisely because E was too high — the Scythians simply moved. Schismogenesis against Achaemenid model.

Characteristics: Palace-centred redistribution economy; Linear A script (undeciphered); no evidence of military fortification until late; possible female ritual authority; thalassocracy
notes: Important pre-Greek Aegean case. Palace as administrative hub without obvious military sovereignty. A relatively high via Linear A record-keeping.

Characteristics: First pan-Indian state; Arthashastra bureaucratic manual; extensive spy network; standardised weights and measures; Ashoka's edicts as administrative communication
notes: Near-maximum A case. Kautilya's Arthashastra is the most explicit pre-modern manual for administrative domination. E and D near zero at peak.

Characteristics: Competitive peer polities; elaborate calendrical ritual; ruler portraiture and dynastic history; warfare as ritual and prestige; no tributary empire; city-state scale
notes: Extreme P case: politics as ritual performance and dynastic display. The 'Terminal Classic collapse' is a Turchin-type elite competition breakdown — P destroys itself when competitive politics becomes unsustainable.
succession_changes: hereditary->hereditary (800): collapse — Classic Maya collapse; individual cities fragment; L drops (0.60→0.30); within-type but key L-conditional case | hereditary->elective (900): reconsolidation — Postclassic council governance (Chichen Itza multepal system)

Characteristics: Largest pre-Columbian city (~125000); standardised apartment compounds suggesting enforced equality; no named rulers in art; murals show collective not royal themes; multi-ethnic
notes: G&W's key large-scale egalitarian urbanism case in Americas. The apartment compounds and absence of ruler iconography are the primary evidence. Violent collapse c.550 CE.

Characteristics: Federated city-state republic; elected council of four lords; resisted Aztec incorporation for 130 years; maintained internal autonomy; allied with Cortés for independence
notes: The counter-case to Aztec. Explicitly maintained autonomy against the regional hegemon — high schismogenesis value. D and E meaningfully higher than surrounding imperial systems.
succession_changes: elective->elective (1521): external_shock — Spanish alliance; council system preserved under Spanish colonial rule; D decreases but election mechanism survives

Characteristics: Five (later six) nations; Great Law of Peace (Gayanashagowa); clan mothers select and can deselect chiefs; consensus decision-making; condolence ritual for leadership transition
notes: G&W's key large-scale egalitarian case for the Americas. Directly influenced Enlightenment political thought (Lahontan, Rousseau). Clan mothers' veto is institutionalised D. Merged with duplicate entry "Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Great Law of Peace".
succession_changes: consensus->consensus (1722): external_shock — Tuscarora admission; sixth nation added; consensus mechanism preserved | consensus->elective (1848): elite_reform — written constitution introduces elected chiefs alongside traditional sachems

Characteristics: Deliberately stateless hill peoples; swidden agriculture (anti-legibility); oral tradition (anti-administration); frequent relocation; ethnic diversity as state-avoidance strategy
notes: James Scott's 'The Art of Not Being Governed' — communities that actively chose statelessness by keeping surplus illegible and maintaining mobility. Maximum E case.
succession_changes: consensus->consensus (1950): external_shock — state incorporation campaigns (Burma, China, Laos, Vietnam); D suppressed; consensus forms persist in highland pockets

Characteristics: Vast bureaucratic empire without writing; quipu knotted-cord accounting; mit'a labour tax; road network; no money; state redistribution of all surplus
notes: High-A without writing — the quipu system is the most sophisticated non-literate administrative apparatus known. Demonstrates L and I can be high without literacy. Merged with duplicate entry "Inca Tawantinsuyu".

Characteristics: Theatre state; ritual as governance; kings competed through ceremony not taxation; no extraction apparatus; power demonstrated through spectacular ritual
notes: Clifford Geertz's 'Negara' — the extreme P anchor. Governance as performance. Geertz argued taxation and coercion were secondary to ritual prestige. Near-zero S and A.

Characteristics: Outdoor assemblies; law-speaker recites law from memory; freeborn men participate; outlawry as primary sanction (enforced exit rather than imprisonment); jury-like panels
notes: The sanction of outlawry is theoretically interesting: the punishment for lawbreaking is enforced E (you must leave). Treats exit as weapon rather than right.
succession_changes: elective->hereditary (1000): external_shock — Christianisation + Harald Fairhair consolidation; thing survives but kings become hereditary with thing ratification ritual

Characteristics: Acephalous; no chiefs; lineage segments balance against each other; tar (land) held communally; disputes resolved by balanced opposition; market exchange without state
notes: Classic Evans-Pritchard/Bohannan stateless society. The opposition structure means any attempted hierarchy is automatically balanced by opposing lineage. Structural D.

Characteristics: Escaped slave communities; rebuilt governance from multiple African traditions; treaty recognition of autonomy; mixed African-Indigenous governance forms
notes: Unique case: explicit reconstruction of E, D, and R by people who had experienced near-zero values of all three. High R by definition — they invented their governance.

Characteristics: Cosmopolitan empire; examination system meritocracy; equal-field land system; complex legal code; tributary system; multiple ethnic groups integrated
notes: Near-maximum SAP anchor case. The examination system is institutionalised P channelled into A. High I via legal codes. Fragmentation in An Lushan rebellion shows resilience threshold breach.
succession_changes: hereditary->hereditary (755): collapse — An Lushan Rebellion; jiedushi fragment central authority; L collapses regionally (0.90→0.40) | hereditary->appointment (960): reconsolidation — Song dynasty restores central keju examination system

Characteristics: Millet system (partial communal autonomy); devshirme slave-soldiers; extensive legal plurality; janissary corps as competitive politics channel; waqf as semi-autonomous civil society
notes: High-SAP but with deliberate E preservation via millet system — a partial exemption from full lock-in. The devshirme is institutionalised P (slave competition for position). R revised upward from 0.25 to 0.45 after reliability exercise: millet system allowing religious communities to govern under own law is a genuine institutional arrangement freedom. Still constrained overall but materially higher than original coding.
succession_changes: hereditary->hereditary (1512): internal — kafes system; princes imprisoned rather than killed; succession violence reduced but hereditary principle unchanged | hereditary->appointment (1826): elite_reform — Mahmud II destroys janissary corps; modern conscript army on merit appointments

Characteristics: Age-set rotational democracy; egalitarian; periodic power transfer; generation-classes rotate through defined roles; built-in leadership term limits
notes: Maximum D anchor: the Gadaa system structurally requires power transfer. Authority cannot be held indefinitely — the age-set rotation is institutionalised D.
succession_changes: rotation->rotation (1800): external_shock — Oromo expansion into highland Ethiopia; gadaa system contracts geographically but rotation mechanism preserved | rotation->hereditary (1900): external_shock — Ethiopian imperial incorporation; gadaa suppressed; hereditary Amhara administration imposed

Characteristics: Clan-based authority; matrilineal inheritance; councils of elders; stool as symbol of community soul; destoolment (removal) of chiefs possible
Acephalous (non-centralized); democratic village councils; age grades; title societies; no permanent chiefs; ofo staff as distributed authority symbol
Council of elders; tribal consensus; tamyiz (distinction/wisdom) as leadership basis; no permanent authority; women excluded but present in some regions
Centralised military monarchy under Shaka; regimental system (amabutho); age-regiment discipline; rapid territorial expansion; no civilian administrative apparatus
notes: Extreme S with minimal A — pure sovereignty without bureaucracy. The amabutho regiments are P channelled into military S rather than administration.

Characteristics: Direct citizen rule; sortition for offices; jury courts; assembly (ekklesia); exclusion of women/slaves/foreigners; ostracism as levelling mechanism
Rule by noble elites; genos (clan) as basis; Areopagus as council; hereditary
succession_changes: hereditary->appointment (-600): elite_reform — Solonian reforms; property-census replaces birth as qualification

Characteristics: Rule by wealthy non-nobles; property qualifications; smaller councils
succession_changes: appointment->elective (-508): elite_reform — Cleisthenic reforms; demokratia replaces oligarchic council | appointment->elective (-403): elite_reform — Thrasybulus; democracy restored after Thirty Tyrants

Characteristics: Balanced mixed government; combining elements of oligarchy and democracy
Rule by spectacle and performance; theatre as political space; demagogues as performers
notes: G&W's Theatrocracy: extreme P with democratic form. The demagogue as performer makes politics a competitive display rather than deliberation.

Characteristics: Rule by military class; Sparta as exemplar; agoge as formation system
Dual kingship with limited checks; Spartan two-king system with ephors
Wealth-based voting rights; Solonian reforms; property classes
Equal power among citizens; Periclean democracy ideal
Direct democracy through referenda; cantonal autonomy; militia army; Landsgemeinde open-air assembly; strong subsidiarity
notes: Merged with duplicate entry "Swiss Cantonal Militias".

Characteristics: Assembly of tribal leaders to elect khan; Yasa law code; dissolved if khan consensus failed; mobile governance
notes: High E even with high S — the kurultai could withdraw legitimacy. The Mongol case shows high-S nomadic governance where exit remains structurally possible.
succession_changes: elective->hereditary (1229): internal — Ögedei succession contested; kurultai increasingly rubber-stamps dynastic line | hereditary->elective (1260): collapse — Toluid Civil War; kurultai authority briefly restored before Kublai consolidates

Characteristics: Elected military leader (Hetman); Cossack council (Rada); annual elections; right of petition; abolished by Catherine II
Conflict-resolution through ritual combat and festivals; dual moiety organisation; balance as political principle
Theocratic rule by priesthood devoted to rain deity; Monte Albán as urban centre; early writing; calendrical administration
Rotating council authority; consensus-building; religious integration; kiva as governance space; seasonal ceremonial calendar
Hierarchical theocratic societies; mound complexes as power display; chiefly redistribution; ranked clans; Cahokia as peak (~15000 pop)
Monarch-led hierarchical tribute system spanning Pacific islands; sacred Tu'i Tonga as divine sovereign; mana as legitimacy; tribute redistribution
notes: Merged with duplicate entry "Tongan Maritime Tui Empire".

Characteristics: Wealth-based council using massive stone disks (rai stones); stones too large to move; consensus required; inter-island tribute network
notes: The rai stone money is theoretically interesting: maximum I (everyone knows who owns what) with minimum physical coercion. Pure social credit system.

Characteristics: Temporary governance for dispute resolution and ceremony; song duels for conflict resolution; no permanent chiefs; seasonal aggregation
notes: Song duels as institutionalised D — disputes resolved through competitive performance rather than coercion. Extreme E due to nomadic lifestyle. Merged with duplicate entry "Inuit Qaggiq Ice-Block Parliaments".

Characteristics: Sacred law-keeping through oral tradition; Dreamtime authority; kinship system as governance; men's and women's separate law; country as jurisdiction
notes: Longest-continuous governance system in dataset. Low population density maintains high E. Sacred law as distributed D — authority is in the Law not the person.

Characteristics: Overlapping suzerainty with tributary states; mandala as concentric sovereignty; local rulers maintain autonomy at periphery; maritime trade basis

Feudal military government under Shogun; sankin-kōtai hostage system (daimyo alternate residences, leaving family as guarantors); strict class hierarchy (shi-nō-kō-shō); sakoku isolation policy; Confucian legitimation; rice-tax economic base
notes: Merged from three upstream entries (Tokugawa Shogunate, Tokugawa Bakufu, Tokugawa Sankin-Kotai). S=0.80: effective coercive monopoly but layered through daimyo, samurai, village headmen rather than direct. A=0.75: cadastral surveys, temple registration, village registers, status categories, rice-tax assessment, road checkpoints, domain bureaucracy. P=0.45 (revised from 0.25): daimyo rank, court titles, alternate attendance processions, castle towns, gift exchange, samurai honour culture, ritualised hierarchy constitute substantial prestige politics. E=0.05: peasants/artisans/merchants/samurai embedded in hereditary status, village/domain obligations, travel restrictions, household registration. D=0.15 (revised from 0.05): peasant petitions, village negotiation, urban protest, domain politics existed within limits — not zero. R=0.15 (revised from 0.05): commercialisation, proto-industrial villages, merchant networks, intellectual schools created some room — constrained but not zero. Binding=land/hostage/status: sankin-kōtai and Edo family residence defining for elite; status law defining for commoners. Collapse=transition (revised from forced opening/reform): overthrown and transformed into Meiji imperial state; Perry arrival was trigger not cause.
succession_changes: hereditary->hereditary (1868): external_shock — Perry + Satsuma-Choshu; succession type unchanged but A/I/L rise sharply under Meiji

Characteristics: Meritocratic civil service system; examination system as P-channel; Mandate of Heaven as D-mechanism (in theory); imperial censorate
notes: Maximum A case. The examination system institutionalises P into A. The Mandate of Heaven is a theoretical D (bad rulers can be overthrown) that rarely functions in practice.
succession_changes: appointment->hereditary (1644): external_shock — Manchu conquest; Qing emperors inherit Confucian bureaucracy but impose hereditary Manchu bannerman overlay | hereditary->appointment (1905): elite_reform — Qing abolish keju; Western-style schools replace examination (transitional)

Characteristics: Elective monarchy with regional autonomy; prince-electors; imperial diet; free cities; complex overlapping jurisdictions
succession_changes: elective->elective (1356): elite_reform — Golden Bull; seven electors codified; electoral principle formalised | elective->hereditary (1438): internal — Habsburg monopoly on throne; election becomes formality

Characteristics: Regional governors under central monarch; tribute collection; local law maintained; royal road communication; Zoroastrian legitimation
Federated system under the Asantehene with regional chiefs; Golden Stool as sacred sovereignty; asafo military companies; slave trade integration
notes: Merged with duplicate entry "Ashanti Golden Stool Confederacy".
